did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning approach involving Vision Transformers (ViT) for visual feature extraction and a dense encoder for clinical metadata, fused via multi-head self-attention, to classify skin diseases (Actinic Keratosis and Basal Cell Carcinoma) using smartphone images from the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new end-to-end deep learning system (hybrid ViT and metadata encoder) for skin disease classification. It details specific methods (multimodal fusion, data preprocessing), reports original results (92.83% accuracy on PAD-UFES-20 dataset), and includes a literature review section but is primarily focused on presenting new technical contributions and validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning for Skin Disease Classification Using Smartphone Images and Clinical Metadata
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Recent Advances in Computing and Systems (REACS)
brief_summary: This study proposes an end-to-end deep learning system to classify Actinic Keratosis and Basal Cell Carcinoma using smartphone images and clinical metadata from the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. The proposed hybrid architecture employs Vision Transformers (ViT) for visual feature extraction and a dense encoder for clinical metadata, which are fused via a Multi-Head Self-Attention module. The model was evaluated on the test dataset of the PAD-UFES-20 dataset to assess its diagnostic performance. The proposed multimodal model achieved a classification accuracy of 92.83% and an F1-score of 90%, outperforming the ViT-only baseline which achieved 90.1% accuracy. These results demonstrate that integrating structured clinical metadata with visual data improves diagnostic accuracy and model interpretability for skin disease classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two clinically relevant skin cancer subtypes classified in the study using the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.
One of the two clinically relevant skin cancer subtypes classified in the study using the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin disease subtypes: Actinic Keratosis (ACK) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'classify two clinically relevant skin cancer subtypes, Actinic Keratosis (ACK) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC).' The model uses a softmax output layer to predict probability distributions over these classes and is trained with categorical cross-entropy loss. It achieves 92.83% accuracy and 90% F1-score on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset containing smartphone images of skin lesions accompanied by structured clinical metadata (including age, sex, lesion location, symptoms, etc.). The study utilized a filtered subset of this dataset focusing on Actinic Keratosis (ACK) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC).

use.training: Used for training the hybrid Vision Transformer and metadata encoder model, with data augmentation applied to classes with lower counts to handle class imbalance.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's diagnostic performance, achieving 92.83% accuracy.
use.other: The raw metadata was sourced from the 'filtered_ack_bcc_metadata.csv' file within the dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Outlier treatment for key numerical features (age, diameter_1, fitspatrick, etc.) by clipping values outside 1.5×IQR to reduce skew.
Normalization of numerical features in the preprocessing pipeline.
Encoding of categorical variables in the preprocessing pipeline.
Handling missing values for continuous numerical variables.
Handling missing values for categorical variables.
Evaluation of model performance on smartphone images and metadata.
Assessment of class-wise classification performance, highlighting true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives.
Assessment of discriminatory power, reporting an AUC of approximately 0.89 for the multimodal fusion model.
Assessment of model effectiveness in scenarios involving class imbalance, reporting an AUC of 0.97.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, F1-score, AUC, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

PAD-UFES-20.experiment: Binary classification of Actinic Keratosis (ACK) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC)
PAD-UFES-20.notes: The proposed multimodal model outperformed the ViT-only baseline. The confusion matrix showed the majority of samples were correctly classified with minimal false positives. The model demonstrated high discriminatory power and effectiveness in scenarios involving class imbalance.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Multimodal Model combining Vision Transformer (ViT) and Clinical Metadata Encoder with Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA) fusion
architecture_used.pretraining: Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) pre-trained on ImageNet-21k dataset
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels (images); Clinical metadata features (age, sex, lesion location, symptoms, etc.)

architecture_details.visual_processing: Images divided into fixed-size patches, embedded with positional encodings, passed through multiple self-attention layers
architecture_details.visual_feature_extraction: Compact feature vector extracted from the pre-logits layer of the fine-tuned ViT
architecture_details.metadata_encoder: Dense encoder (fully connected layers with non-linear activations) with dropout to prevent overfitting
architecture_details.metadata_preprocessing: One-hot encoding for categorical variables, min-max scaling for continuous variables, median/mode imputation for missing values, Interquartile Range (IQR) for outlier treatment
architecture_details.fusion_mechanism: Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA) module with 8 attention heads
architecture_details.fusion_input: Concatenation of visual embeddings from ViT and high-dimensional metadata embeddings

training_details.data_augmentation: Random rotations, flips, shearing, zooming, rescaling, resized cropping, and normalization for images
training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Data augmentation for classes with lower counts; potential use of class weighting mentioned as a possibility

classification_or_output_head.components: Normalization, fully connected layers, ReLU activation, and dropout
classification_or_output_head.output: Probability distribution over two classes (Actinic Keratosis and Basal Cell Carcinoma)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed Hybrid Multimodal Model (Vision Transformer ViT-B/16 + Dense Metadata Encoder + Multi-Head Self-Attention)
Deep multimodal fusion model with parallel CNNs (Ou et al.)
Skin tumor classifier combining DenseNet and patient metadata (Yin et al.)
CNN with soft-attention mechanisms (Datta et al.)
Joint-Individual Fusion Structure (JIFS) with Fusion Attention Module (Tang et al.)
Hybrid CNN and Transformer model with focal loss (Nie et al.)
Simple CNN with BatchNorm, MaxPooling, and Dense layers
preprocessing_techniques: Image data augmentation (random rotations, flips, shearing, zooming, rescaling, resized cropping)
Metadata one-hot encoding for categorical variables
Metadata min-max scaling for continuous variables
Metadata median imputation for missing continuous values
Metadata mode imputation for missing categorical values
Metadata Interquartile Range (IQR) outlier treatment (clipping values outside 1.5×IQR)
Metadata StandardScaler normalization for numerical features
Metadata feature engineering (deriving diameter_ratio, diameter_area, age_group, symptom_count)
Metadata handling of missing indicators (binary indicator for missing diameter, 'UNK' token for ambiguous categorical entries)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to classes with lower counts to handle class imbalance and improve generalizability. Specific techniques included random rotations, flips, shearing, zooming, and rescaling.
Applied as a data augmentation technique to improve generalization.
Included to simulate intra-class variability and mitigate class imbalance.
Mentioned as a potential method to handle class imbalance bias during training, though specific weights were not reported.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning model, which fuses Vision Transformer (ViT) visual features with clinical metadata via Multi-Head Self-Attention, achieved a classification accuracy of 92.83% and an F1-score of 90% on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.
Integrating structured clinical metadata with smartphone images improves diagnostic performance compared to using visual data alone, as the ViT-only baseline achieved 90.1% accuracy.
The multimodal approach enhances model interpretability and generalization to test data, demonstrating the value of combining image and non-image data streams for robust skin disease classification.
The proposed system shows large potential for applications in teledermatology and under-resourced healthcare systems due to its use of smartphone images and structured patient data.
The study highlights that using clinical patient data in addition to visual data results in a better intelligent diagnosis for Actinic Keratosis and Basal Cell Carcinoma.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Actinic Keratosis vs. Basal Cell Carcinoma) using a filtered subset of the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, lacking generalizability to other skin diseases or broader dermatological contexts.
The model was evaluated exclusively on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset without external validation on independent, heterogeneous, or prospective clinical datasets, limiting evidence of real-world robustness.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported to validate the statistical significance of the performance improvement over baselines.
The paper acknowledges that the model requires optimization for computational burden to enable real-time application on mobile and wearable devices, indicating current deployment constraints.
Class imbalance was addressed via data augmentation and potential class weighting, but specific class weights or detailed imbalance metrics were not fully reported.
The study highlights the need for prospective validation studies with healthcare professionals to assess clinical impact, interpretability, and trustworthiness before clinical adoption.
